EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that several trees were downed in Gamaliel and other locations around Monroe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain brought two tenths of an inch of glaze to northern Bullitt County before changing over to sleet and snow. Two to 3 inches of snow fell later in the afternoon across northern Bullitt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds increased ahead of a cold front approaching from Missouri. Sustained winds were mostly from 15 to 30 mph, with gusts from 35 to 50 mph. Several locations measured gusts over 45 mph. Those locations included the mesonet site near Cadiz (in Trigg County), which measured a gust to 50 mph. The automated observing site at Fort Campbell near Hopkinsville measured a gust to 48 mph. Wind gusts to 46 mph were measured at the Henderson airport and the mesonet site near Madisonville. Wind gusts to 45 mph were measured at the Madisonville airport and the mesonet site in Mclean County near Calhoun.

South winds increased ahead of a cold front approaching from Missouri. Sustained winds were mostly from 15 to 30 mph, with gusts from 35 to 50 mph. Several locations measured gusts over 45 mph. Those locations included the mesonet site near Cadiz (in Trigg County), which measured a gust to 50 mph. The automated observing site at Fort Campbell near Hopkinsville measured a gust to 48 mph. Wind gusts to 46 mph were measured at the Henderson airport and the mesonet site near Madisonville. Wind gusts to 45 mph were measured at the Madisonville airport and the mesonet site in Mclean County near Calhoun.

Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

Low pressure drew an unseasonably warm and moist air mass across the region. Convection organized ahead of the low and brought heavy rainfall and damaging winds to the area from the evening of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rushing water due to heavy rainfall ripped up pavement on Highway 467 between Highway 35 and Route 127.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via Twitter of a tree downed across Old Sheep Pen Road west of Frankfort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced two tenths of an inch of glaze near Fort Knox.  Freezing rain later changed to sleet and snow, with another one to two inches of accumulation along the Ohio River in the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported by down by estimated 70 mph winds.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.

An arctic cold front swept through during the early morning hours on the 1st.  The on and off snow showers, that fell during Jan 31st, became a steadier snow after 0000E.  The snow diminished to snow showers by dawn.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common in northeast Kentucky.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several outbuildings were damaged or destroyed. A dog kennel was picked up and destroyed. Several shallow-rooted trees were uprooted, and one tree was snapped. A carport was destroyed. There was minor roof damage to some homes, mostly shingles blown off. Porch columns on a home were shifted. There was light damage to a total of nine homes along State Route 94. Two car windows were broken by falling tree limbs. A power pole was snapped at the intersection of Highways 94 and 783. The average path width was 50 yards. Peak winds were estimated near 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak winds in the tornado were estimated near 120 mph. The average damage path width was about 150 yards. Three homes sustained major damage. A two-story Amish home had its entire roof system removed, and a window was blown out. A block wall milk barn was levelled except for part of the north wall. A machine shop was unroofed, and a barn was levelled. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted. Trained spotters observed light debris in the air near Herndon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along U.S. Highway 41A and travelled northeast to U.S. Highway 41. Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. One grain bin was blown 150 feet. Several barns or sheds were levelled. At least two dozen power poles were broken along Highway 1027 and U.S. Highway 41. Several homes received mainly minor roof damage. The tornado had estimated peak winds of 105 mph. The average path width was 175 yards.

Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Fordsville reported freezing rain with ice accretions of three tenths of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Green Briar Loop.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds were estimated near 120 mph. The average path width was 275 yards. Large portions of the roof structure were removed from one home, including rafters. Several other homes lost varying numbers of shingles. Siding was damaged on several homes, along with a few destroyed porches. Two mobile homes were completely destroyed, including one mobile home that was overturned. Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. Power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on a residential property in the Indian Hills Subdivision.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey confirmed a tornado that damaged the northern portion of Campbellsville. It touched down just west of the intersection of US 68 and Ky 323, where it destroyed several outbuildings. After crossing KY 210, it uprooted trees and damaged several buildings near the intersection of Hodgenville Road and Vintage Lane.  Damage rated as EF-1 was surveyed as the tornado crossed Saloma Road and moved roughly parallel to and just north of Upper Miller Park Road.  Several trees were toppled and several rooftops were damaged.  After crossing the intersection of Eastern Drive and East Lake Drive, it brought additional roof and tree damage to locations near the intersection of US 68 and Palestine Road. At this point, the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported that freezing rain deposited one third of an inch of ice by late morning across western Grayson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds increased ahead of a cold front approaching from Missouri. Sustained winds were mostly from 15 to 30 mph, with gusts from 35 to 50 mph. Several locations measured gusts over 45 mph. Those locations included the mesonet site near Cadiz (in Trigg County), which measured a gust to 50 mph. The automated observing site at Fort Campbell near Hopkinsville measured a gust to 48 mph. Wind gusts to 46 mph were measured at the Henderson airport and the mesonet site near Madisonville. Wind gusts to 45 mph were measured at the Madisonville airport and the mesonet site in Mclean County near Calhoun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog reduced visibility to as low as 200 feet in the Hopkinsville area. The dense fog occurred beneath a strong high pressure ridge that extended from western Kentucky to the upper Mississippi Valley.

A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across the Bluegrass Region on the 5th and the 6th (in advance of the cold air intrusion that occurred on the 6th) led to minor river flooding at Paris. Stoner Creek rose to 18.5 feet during the late evening on the 6th. Flood state is 16 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via Twitter that two barns were destroyed near Western Elementary School.  Several signs were twisted as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down on Hwy 191.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

Areas of dense fog reduced visibility to as low as 200 feet in the Hopkinsville area. The dense fog occurred beneath a strong high pressure ridge that extended from western Kentucky to the upper Mississippi Valley.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home sustained minor roof damage. A shed was destroyed. A barn roof was blown into a field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall led to flooding across eastern Kentucky on December 6th and 7th. In general, between two and half and three and a half inches of rain fell for the event and a number of roads around the area were underwater from time to time. There were also a few instances of automobiles becoming surrounded by water.  The rain transitioned to a wintry mix late on the 6th before ending and this led to a few automobile accidents in Fleming county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low water bridge at Round Bottom Road just off Hwy 30 was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey confirmed widespread wind damage associated with a squall line around Cave City.  Several businesses had signs blown down or damaged.  A 60 foot tall Super 8 sign next to Interstate 65 was toppled. Trees and power lines were downed.  A grocery store had structural damage, while several other businesses received roof damage.  Wind damage was noted north of Cave City along US 31, where Alvey's Towing Recovery was damaged.  Surveyors estimated straight line winds of between 65 and 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm damage survey team from the National Weather Service office in Jackson, KY confirmed an EF-0 tornado in Pulaski County around 630 am EST this morning, January 30th. Touchdown occurred in the Ferguson Community near the end of the runway of the Lake Cumberland Regional Airport. The tornado traveled a half mile through a residential area crossing Murphy Avenue near the Gover Lane intersection. Some roof and siding damage was noted to structures along the path. Several trees were also downed along the path and one tree fell across and destroyed a shed.  This is the first documented tornado to occur during the month of January in the National Weather Service Jackson, KY forecast area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal band of fast moving showers and thunderstorms trained over northeast Kentucky from 0630E through 1300E.  The maximum observed rainfall was on the order of 1.25 inches in 3 hours, with 1.25 to 2.0 inches falling in 6 hours.   The maximum amount measured was 2.01 inches from Carter Caves in about 6 hours.  ||With the ground wet from previous rains and snow melt, plus the lack of spring vegetation, many streams overflowed, causing minor flooding.  Water blocked and closed roads.  Water also surrounded a few buildings.  No evacuations or damage to structures were reported. ||The stream gauge on Tygart Creek rose some 15 feet in the vertical in response to the rain, to near bankfull.  The Little Sandy River rose over 13 feet in the vertical at Grayson, but remained below flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams overflowed and blocked roads in scattered locations across much of the county.  Route 60 was closed by the headwaters of Tygart Creek.  Route 174 was closed heading south out of Olive Hill.  Cory Branch flooded Sammons Hollow Road.

Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 74 mph was measured at the mesonet site near Princeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along a couple of rivers in western Kentucky. The rivers rose above flood stage in response to the heavy rain event on the 12th and 13th, followed by some lighter wintry precipitation events during the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Ohio River near Uniontown. Some low-lying fields and woodlands near the river were underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm developed along a quasi-stationary front that extended from southwest to northeast along the Lower Ohio River Valley. Deep and strong southwesterly wind flow stretched from Texas to the Great Lakes region ahead of an expansive upper-level trough over the western and central states. Several weak disturbances tracked northeastward, contributing to the development of the isolated supercell. Strong low and mid-level wind fields supported the formation of the supercell, which spawned an isolated EF-2 tornado in Livingston County.|Later on during the night, more storms developed in a region of strong warm advection near a low level jet in excess of 50 knots. Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medium size tree limbs were blown down. A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 60 mph.

A repair shop was unroofed, and several other barns and shops were partially unroofed. Dozens of trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow aloft brought increasing moisture to the Commonwealth during the early morning hours. An inverted trough developed across Tennessee and became the focus for an expanding area of wintry precipitation across central Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours on December 8th. After a brief bout of sleet, precipitation changed to freezing rain. An icy glaze developed across central Kentucky, with ice accumulations of up to one quarter of an inch along a line from east of Bowling Green northeast to Lincoln County. North of this line, up to one inch of snow fell in Louisville and other locations along Interstate 64 towards Frankfort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain deposited 2 tenths of an inch of glaze on elevated surfaces near Scottsville. This ice toppled a weakened tree onto a home, damaging its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A reporter from the Hawesville newspaper provided information about a home on Goering Road that was badly damaged by winds estimated at 70 mph. The home lost its front porch and a section of an attached roof. A nearby barn was destroyed. The house's attached garage had its door blown in, with a section of the garage's back wall bowed out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey confirmed widespread damage across southern Hart County east of Horse Cave consistent with straight line winds of 70 to 80 mph. A squall line moved across the area, flipping and destroying a single-wide mobile home just east of Horse Cave. Several barns were destroyed along Highway 218. Many trees were downed as well. On Bunnell Crossing Road near Pascal, a dairy silo was toppled and a barn was extensively damaged. Metal sheeting was removed from this barn and blown downwind for 400 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system and associated arctic boundary brought snow showers and snow squalls to eastern Kentucky from Thursday Evening January 31st, to the predawn hours of Friday February 1st. The snow came down quickly, reducing the visibility to below a quarter of a mile at times. When combined with blowing already fallen snow, near whiteout conditions were briefly witnessed. Thundersnow was also witnessed in locations such as Hazard and Paintsville. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches over the lower elevations with 4 to 6 inches in isolated locations on the higher ridges along the Virginia border. Winds gusted up to 47 mph as the arctic front blew through causing several hundred power outages across eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from a little over 2 inches in Harlan up to 6 inches on Hwy 160 over Black Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof was partially blown off on Highway 286 near the U.S. 62 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall led to flooding across eastern Kentucky on December 6th and 7th. In general, between two and half and three and a half inches of rain fell for the event and a number of roads around the area were underwater from time to time. There were also a few instances of automobiles becoming surrounded by water.  The rain transitioned to a wintry mix late on the 6th before ending and this led to a few automobile accidents in Fleming county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chloe Road was shut down due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rains of around 2 inches fell across central Kentucky during the evening hours on Dec 21st. This led to minor flooding on the Rough River at Dundee. The river crested at 25.1 feet just before midnight on the 22nd.  Flood state is 25 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal band of fast moving showers and thunderstorms trained over northeast Kentucky from 0630E through 1300E.  The maximum observed rainfall was on the order of 1.25 inches in 3 hours, with 1.25 to 2.0 inches falling in 6 hours.   The maximum amount measured was 2.01 inches from Carter Caves in about 6 hours.  ||With the ground wet from previous rains and snow melt, plus the lack of spring vegetation, many streams overflowed, causing minor flooding.  Water blocked and closed roads.  Water also surrounded a few buildings.  No evacuations or damage to structures were reported. ||The stream gauge on Tygart Creek rose some 15 feet in the vertical in response to the rain, to near bankfull.  The Little Sandy River rose over 13 feet in the vertical at Grayson, but remained below flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams overflowed and blocked roads in scattered locations in much of the county.  Little Hood Creek flooded the bridge at the intersection of Iowa and Pritchard in Westwood.  Another example, was along Trace Fork and Rush Creek.  They flooded roads.  Water surrounded a few buildings.

No dwellings were flooded.  Sandsuck Creek flooded roads and into yards of residents near Route 207.   Chinns Branch flooded southwest of Raceland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported via HAM radio that KY HWY 60 was under water near the Dollar Store in Irvington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall led to flooding across eastern Kentucky on December 6th and 7th. In general, between two and half and three and a half inches of rain fell for the event and a number of roads around the area were underwater from time to time. There were also a few instances of automobiles becoming surrounded by water.  The rain transitioned to a wintry mix late on the 6th before ending and this led to a few automobile accidents in Fleming county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the road on KY 550 at WKCB radio station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking to our south provided a shot of snow to parts of extreme southeast Kentucky on Thursday January 17th. There was a sharp cutoff to the precipitation, but where it fell, it fell heavily for a 2 to 3 hour period. The snow was even accompanied by thunder in parts of Bell and Harlan Counties during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 of snow was reported from near Pineville south to Middlesboro with numerous accidents reported on Highway 25E, 119 and 190.

However, 6 to 12 inches of snow accumulated over the higher terrain along the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches in the valleys, although much of that melted on contact with the ground, up to 4.4 inches on the ridge at the NWS Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Highway 11 and a limb from the tree damaged a school bus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell in Milton. Six inches of snow fell along the Ohio River on the northern border of Trimble County. Freezing rain and sleet produced a thin glaze prior to changing over to snow.

Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain deposited around 2 tenths of an inch of ice before changing over to sleet and snow. By late afternoon 3.2 inches of sleet and snow were measured near Buckner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow showers that occurred along and north of the I64 corridor on December 16th created slick roads that lead to numerous car accidents in the area. Even though only two tenths of an inch of snow fell, it fell fast enough to accumulate on roadways and create very hazardous driving conditions in Rowan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rowan county emergency manager reported that I64 was closed at mile marker 135 due to slick roads and several accidents that were caused by icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two systems, one tracking through the Ohio Valley and another moving across the southern Appalachians combined to bring snow and wintry precipitation to eastern Kentucky from shortly after midnight through the mid morning hours on Tuesday December 10th. The heaviest totals received were from Pike and Letcher counties where as much as 3 to 4 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported around four inches of snow fell in the Kimper and Dorton areas of Pike County.  Elsewhere, two to three inches were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Hwy 1419.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey found that several hardwood trees were downed along Pleasant Valley Road near the intersection with Coakley Road. Wind speeds were estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive intrusion of arctic air brought near record cold to the Rockies and the northern Plains by December 5th. Late in the day, strong southwesterly flow aloft brought ample moisture along the leading edge of this arctic airmass, which, by now extended from central Texas through Indiana.  Widespread mixed wintry precipitation developed all along the boundary, aided by upper level support from the right entrance region of a strong jet.  Across northern Kentucky, freezing rain developed during the mid-morning hours and deposited up to one quarter of an inch of ice across west central Kentucky, west of Interstate 65.  Just south of the Ohio River, freezing rain turned to sleet and deposited up to one half inch.  Sleet changed to snow by early afternoon, depositing an additional 4 inches to locations along the Ohio River. Across the Bluegrass, heavy rain on the 5th and 6th brought minor flooding to Stoner Creek in Bourbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of ice, sleet and snow were reported across Henry County. Two tenths of an inch of ice were followed by 2 to 3 inches of sleet and snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on the 29th of January, low pressure across the southern plains began to intensify and move towards the western Great Lakes. A squall line developed during the evening hours and strengthened after midnight as it moved into western Kentucky and southern Illinois. This line, fueled by unseasonably mild temperatures near 70 degrees, dewpoints around 60, and an intense low level jet of 80kt at 850mb, spawned one of the largest January tornadic outbreaks on record across the Lower Ohio Valley. This line entered west central Kentucky around 2 am January 30th, exiting the Bluegrass and Lake Cumberland Regions during the pre-dawn hours. Several circulations along the squall line produced tornadoes. In addition, strong synoptic southerly winds right ahead and along this line produced localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey revealed that an EF-0 tornado with estimated 80 mph winds and a width of 200 yards struck Meade County around 5.7 miles southeast of Brandenburg. Initially, this tornado touched down in a grove of trees near the end of Kurtz Drive. It then damaged several homes on Kurtz Drive and near the intersection of Thornhill Drive and Gaines Road.  One owner of a home east of Gaines Road mentioned that water was evacuated from all 4 toilets as the tornado passed by. Local residents reported a loud roar.  Another home along Gaines Road has its front door pulled off.  Before lifting right off Tom Cain Road, the tornado damaged a small shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on January 25, a weak inverted trough associated with the southern branch of the jet stream moved across Tennessee. Ahead of this feature, southerly winds aloft brought a limited amount of moisture northward, overrunning an arctic airmass already in place across the Commonwealth. Light freezing rain began during the early morning hours and overspread southern Kentucky by dawn. Freezing rain fell into very cold dry air with temperatures around 25 degrees, promoting efficient ice accumulations. Overall, rainfall totals ranging from nearly 2 tenths to as much as 4 tenths of an inch brought ice accretions ranging from nearly a tenth of an inch from near Bowling Green eastwards through Casey County to as much as one quarter of an inch over Cumberland and Clinton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain brought ice accumulations of around one quarter of an inch. A Kentucky Mesonet rain gage measured 0.36 hundredths of an inch of rain, the bulk of which fell in a 28 degree environment.

This line, fueled by unseasonably mild temperatures near 70 degrees, dewpoints around 60, and an intense low level jet of 80kt at 850mb, spawned one of the largest January tornadic outbreaks on record across the Lower Ohio Valley. Several circulations along the squall line produced tornadoes. In addition, strong synoptic southerly winds right ahead and along this line produced localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak EF-0 tornado with estimated winds of 80 to 85 mph briefly touched down along Calvary Road around 7 miles southwest of Bradfordsville. Although this twister had a path length estimated of only a tenth of a mile, it did destroy a mobile home, injuring two occupants. It also slid a shed off of its foundation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down just to the east of Interstate 65 around 3 miles southwest of Smith's Grove.  After damaging several outbuildings, it encountered a set of 4 grain silos just to the south of Glasgow Road. It destroyed all 4 silos and threw their remains several hundred yards to the east. The tornado stayed on the ground  for  3 miles, eventually crossing Interstate 65 just south of Smith's Grove. This tornado overturned a semi-trailer on the interstate. Before lifting up just to the east of Smith's Grove, it destroyed an older wooden barn and a large  well constructed dairy barn, ripping steel I-beams out of their concrete base, scattering its roof, and collapsing its southern and eastern cinder block walls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A circulation embedded within a squall line that earlier produced an EF-2 tornado in Warren County spawned a second tornado that briefly touched down in Rocky Hill. It then lifted and touched down again around one mile to the northeast. Along its second track, which was around seven tenths of a mile, it snapped several trees within two  long rows of trees along fence lines. Near the end of its path, along Louisville Road, it tore half the roof off of a home and destroyed a nearby barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably moist air, combined with a nearly stationary boundary draped from Michigan through western Tennessee, lead to several days of heavy rain across west central and central Kentucky. Over a 4 day period from late Thursday, January 10th through Sunday the 14th, 3 to 4 inches of rain were recorded. The highest amounts, around two and one half inches, fell along the Ohio River southwest of Louisville over a 15 hour period ending Sunday afternoon.  This led to some very minor flooding across low water crossings and open fields.  Several rivers experienced minor flooding on Sunday and Monday, January 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official reported that low water crossings along three roads near Franklin were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on January 25, a weak inverted trough associated with the southern branch of the jet stream moved across Tennessee. Ahead of this feature, southerly winds aloft brought a limited amount of moisture northward, overrunning an arctic airmass already in place across the Commonwealth. Light freezing rain began during the early morning hours and overspread southern Kentucky by dawn. Freezing rain fell into very cold dry air with temperatures around 25 degrees, promoting efficient ice accumulations. Overall, rainfall totals ranging from nearly 2 tenths to as much as 4 tenths of an inch brought ice accretions ranging from nearly a tenth of an inch from near Bowling Green eastwards through Casey County to as much as one quarter of an inch over Cumberland and Clinton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain led to a mufti-vehicle accident along Interstate 65 near the Glendale Exit. The interstate was closed for a couple of hours. Around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated on area roads and other surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small, low-topped thunderstorm line segments moved northeast in a region of strong vertical wind shear and very weak instability. These line segments produced a few short-track, weak tornadoes and isolated wind damage. The tornadoes formed from mesovortices embedded within the line. There was little if any lightning associated with the convection. The storms occurred along a cold front that extended southward from a low pressure center in northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weak tornado briefly touched down, removing a few shingles from two homes.  One home had soffit damage. A couple large branches were blown down. Peak winds were estimated near 75 mph. The average path width was about 100 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches were blown down. The average damage path width was 100 yards and the peak winds were 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across southern Indiana during the early morning hours March 18th. Widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across central Kentucky during the early morning hours ahead of a cold front that would approach the Ohio River later that afternoon. Several episodes of thunderstorms formed and moved northeast along an axis across south central Kentucky, roughly stretching from Logan to Casey Counties.  In general, 2 1/2 to 3 inches of rain fell across Warren, Barren, Metcalfe, Allen, Casey and Adair Counties, leading to minor flooding of intersections and low water crossings.  Several days later, several larger main-stem rivers went into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager reported a mudslide that blocked a portion of Tate's Creek Road between Whitlock and Maple Grove Roads.  Around 2 inches of rain fell nearby over a 5 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and north of a slow-moving warm front over western Tennessee, clusters of moderate to heavy rain occurred. These clusters moved eastward across southern Kentucky, where the ground was saturated. Southwest low-level flow on the order of 20 to 30 knots overran the surface warm front. The air mass south of the warm front was characterized by precipitable water values of 1.2 to 1.5 inches. The persistent nature of the very moist southwest flow overrunning the warm front resulted in very heavy convective rainfall along the Tennessee border, mainly from Murray eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 36-hour rainfall total was 4.25 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Highway 1828 was closed due to flooding. A trained spotter in the area measured 1.40 inches of rain in about 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through Kentucky towards southeast Ohio and brought a round of winter weather to much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report near Moranburg and another in Maysville both had 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through Kentucky towards southeast Ohio and brought a round of winter weather to much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches was measured by the observer 3 miles northwest of Walton, and by a spotter in Florence. The CVG airport measured 4.3 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the downtown area of Guthrie. Streets in Guthrie were closed due to high water on them. Some of the streets were not known to flood in the past. Basements of several homes and a couple of businesses were flooded. Water up to 8 inches deep was reported over a 500-yard stretch of U.S. Highway 41 between Trenton and Guthrie. Highway 181 in southern Todd County had up to a foot of water over it in some spots. Up to a foot of water was reported over a couple of other roads. Radar estimated that 3 to 5 inches of rain had fallen over southern Todd County in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was shingle and soffit damage to several homes. Several trees were snapped, and large branches were blown down. The average damage path width was 150 yards, and the peak winds were about 80 mph. The tornado moved into Marshall County near the community of Oak Level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line with embedded small bow echoes moved eastward from southeast Missouri across western Kentucky. A few damaging wind events occurred in association with the bow echoes. The storms occurred in a region of strong southerly low-level wind flow ahead of a cold front crossing Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1000-pound factory awning was blown off, carried by the wind approximately 70 yards, and landed on top of several vehicles. A few of the vehicles were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms moved rapidly east across western Kentucky, producing strong winds and isolated wind damage. The storms occurred in a moist southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front over Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported a tree blown down with an estimated wind gust near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front in the area and a mid level disturbance helped generate an organized band of thunderstorms in Kentucky during the afternoon.  That large cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through eastern Kentucky during the early evening hours.  The low level flow backed around to the southeast.  As a result, a few stronger cells, on the southern edge of this convection, were able to maintain their strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leaves were stripped off of trees.  The driving rain pasted the leaves against outside walls of homes and on vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front in the area and a mid level disturbance helped generate an organized band of thunderstorms in Kentucky during the afternoon.  That large cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through eastern Kentucky during the early evening hours.  The low level flow backed around to the southeast.  As a result, a few stronger cells, on the southern edge of this convection, were able to maintain their strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.  One mobile home had its roof ripped off.  Another house had a section of its roof damaged.  The Walnut Grove Church was also damaged.  Electricity was knocked out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived convective complex originated on the 20th across the southern Plains.  The complex moved across Tennessee and Kentucky during the day on the 21st, reaching northeast Kentucky by early evening.||Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were common on the leading edge of the convection.  A few spots received stronger wind gusts with tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees fell along Routes 1690 and 2037.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms formed over central Kentucky and then pushed eastward into east Kentucky during the morning hours of April 17th. The thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage early in the morning in Letcher County, but the bigger impacts were from flash flooding later in the morning. Flash flooding was reported in Estill, Owsley, Bell, and Harlan Counties, but the worst of it occurred in Clay and Knox Counties. Several people had to be rescued from the rising waters in the Brightshade area of Clay County and along Highway 223 near Scalf in Knox County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several inches of water standing on Blacklog Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front approaching the Ohio River. The activity was fostered by upper-level diffluence overlying a relatively warm moist boundary layer in the presence of 35 to 40 knots of west-southwesterly 700 mb flow. A few of the storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported on the south end of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent disturbance moving across Missouri aided in the development of a nearly stationary front that extended eastward from weak low pressure over Arkansas during the late morning of Sunday, March 24th. Elevated instability, despite surface temperatures in the 40s, led to the development of a broken line of thunderstorms that moved northeast from western Tennessee across south central Kentucky. One storm in particular showed some characteristics of a supercell as it moved into Simpson County, Kentucky. It produce widespread prolific hail that whitened the ground across Simpson and Warren Counties, including the city of Bowling Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across southern Indiana during the early morning hours March 18th. Widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across central Kentucky during the early morning hours ahead of a cold front that would approach the Ohio River later that afternoon. Several episodes of thunderstorms formed and moved northeast along an axis across south central Kentucky, roughly stretching from Logan to Casey Counties.  In general, 2 1/2 to 3 inches of rain fell across Warren, Barren, Metcalfe, Allen, Casey and Adair Counties, leading to minor flooding of intersections and low water crossings.  Several days later, several larger main-stem rivers went into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local media reported water covering Freeport Road in Bowling Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread area of rain persisted across portions of northern Kentucky throughout the morning and into the early afternoon. As a result, heavy rainfall accumulations occurred as well as isolated flooding and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and north of a slow-moving warm front over western Tennessee, clusters of moderate to heavy rain occurred. These clusters moved eastward across southern Kentucky, where the ground was saturated. Southwest low-level flow on the order of 20 to 30 knots overran the surface warm front. The air mass south of the warm front was characterized by precipitable water values of 1.2 to 1.5 inches. The persistent nature of the very moist southwest flow overrunning the warm front resulted in very heavy convective rainfall along the Tennessee border, mainly from Murray eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads were closed by flooding. Water was over numerous other roadways. Highway 121 just south of New Concord was flooded and dangerous. Radar estimated that 3 to 5 inches of rain fell during the previous 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm damage survey team from the National Weather Service office in Jackson, KY confirmed that damage that occurred on Ficklin Road between Camargo and Jeffersonville bewteen 658 and 705 pm EDT on Thursday April 11, was caused by straight line winds. Winds were estimated to have peaked between 85 and 95 mph. The straight line winds spread debris out across an area approximately 660 yards in length and about 720 yards wide. Several homes and barns sustained significant wind damage, with multiple trees being felled as well. Two barns, a small single structure and a few tress also sustained damage from the winds on Camargo Levee Road at around 658 pm EDT. The debris field left on Camargo Levee Road was approximately 400 yards long and 100 yards wide.

Significant wind damage occurred in Montgomery county between Camargo and Jeffersonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was blown down onto a home in the Stulltown community near Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across southern Indiana during the early morning hours March 18th. Widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across central Kentucky during the early morning hours ahead of a cold front that would approach the Ohio River later that afternoon. Several episodes of thunderstorms formed and moved northeast along an axis across south central Kentucky, roughly stretching from Logan to Casey Counties.  In general, 2 1/2 to 3 inches of rain fell across Warren, Barren, Metcalfe, Allen, Casey and Adair Counties, leading to minor flooding of intersections and low water crossings.  Several days later, several larger main-stem rivers went into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Metcalfe County emergency manager reported that 7 county and 2 state roads across the county were impassable due to flooding. The Kentucky Mesonet, located 5 miles west of Edmonton, measured 2.7 inches of rain in 6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central Illinois southward across western Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding problems were observed in the Murray area. Standing water was over many roads, causing traffic hazards. The Kentucky mesonet site at Murray measured a daily rainfall total of 2.98 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent cluster of thunderstorms intensified as it moved into a moist and very unstable air mass over southwest Indiana. The cluster of storms slowly expanded into a large mesoscale convective system that covered southern Illinois, western Kentucky, and the Mississippi River counties of extreme southeast Missouri. The convective system was supported by mid-level forcing associated with a weak disturbance, along with a low-level inflow of warmer air from the southwest. The thunderstorm complex produced gusty winds, small hail, and an isolated funnel cloud over western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime-size hail was reported on U.S. Highway 68 west of Hopkinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms moved north across western Kentucky. The storms occurred in a southwesterly flow of moist and moderately unstable air. The storms were enhanced by a 500 mb shortwave extending from the Ozarks to the Lower Mississippi Valley. A few of the storms produced large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was on a power line west of U.S. Highway 641. Another tree was blown down across Kentucky 94 west of Murray, causing an accident with injuries. A few other trees were blown across roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center moved northeast along the Ohio River. Thunderstorms in the warm sector of this low produced isolated flash flooding in western Kentucky. The storms were aided by an upper-level low over the Great Lakes region with a shortwave trough extending south along the Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapidly rising floodwaters inundated some buildings in Greenville, including a rest home. The rest home was sandbagged after a couple inches of water entered the facility. Some roads in Greenville were flooded. At least one house was isolated by floodwaters in the Belton area, prompting an evacuation. Numerous roads were flooded and impassable in the Beechmont and Belton areas along U.S. Highway 431.  Impassable roads included portions of U.S. Highway 431, as well as State Routes 70, 2270, and 1163. At least one vehicle was stranded. About ten homes were flooded. Luzerne Lake flooded portions of Kentucky Highways 601 and 189. Floodwaters threatened a power substation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and remained quasi-stationary in association with a surface low pressure center over the Mississippi River between St. Louis, Missouri and Cairo, Illinois. The storms were the result of the combination of the surface low, daytime heating, and a very moist atmosphere with precipitable water values approaching two inches. The storms were further enhanced by a 500 mb shortwave trough over southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was up to five inches deep on several roads.

A squall line with embedded small bow echoes moved eastward from southeast Missouri across western Kentucky. A few damaging wind events occurred in association with the bow echoes. The storms occurred in a region of strong southerly low-level wind flow ahead of a cold front crossing Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down near U.S. Highway 62. Utility companies cleared the debris, which affected power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 11th, a line of storms weakened during the morning hours as it moved into west central Kentucky. By early afternoon, the remnants of this line hung up over central Kentucky, stretching from the Bluegrass Region southwest to near Bowling Green. East of this area of showers, partial sun and surface temperatures in the middle 70s lead to weak surface based instability.  By mid-afternoon, thunderstorms developed across east central Kentucky, along the eastern edge of the rain farther west. Some of these storms produced gusty winds and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cumberland County Emergency Manager reported some shingles were blown off several houses near the intersection of Free Ferry and Smith Grove Roads. Some ceder trees were downed and some tin was removed from a barn near the same location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS automated weather gauge at the Berea local airport measured a 61 mph wind gust.  Nearby, in the town of Berea, gusty winds toppled a couple of shallow rooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level jet developed during the evening of April 27 across Tennessee. This jet intersected an elevated warm front and brought widespread thunderstorms to northern Tennessee and the southern two tiers of counties across central Kentucky.  Spotty flooding developed across southern Kentucky, especially near Bowling Green,  where two to three inches of rain fell within a three hour period during the late evening hours. These rains led to subsequent flooding of Drake's Creek near Alvaton, as well as the Barren and Green Rivers, over the next few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported that Three Springs Road was briefly closed due to high water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued from late April into May along the Green River at Paradise. Some low-lying fields and woodlands near the river were inundated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst with winds near 70 mph began near Lone Oak and extended northward to the western edge of Paducah, ending at exit 3 on Interstate 24. The most significant tree damage occurred in the vicinity of U.S. Highway 62, where dozens of trees were uprooted or snapped. Other large trees were uprooted between U.S. 60 and U.S. 62 near the western city limits of Paducah. Near the end of the damage path, a billboard was blown down at exit 3 on Interstate 24. Also at exit 3, the canopy of a former gas station was extensively damaged. The damage path width averaged 500 yards, but was up to 1000 yards wide near U.S. 62.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system that originated over the Missouri Ozarks maintained strength as it moved through the middle Mississippi Valley during the overnight hours. The system slowed down and then stalled as it crossed the Lower Ohio Valley very early in the morning. The stalled thunderstorm complex produced major flash flooding across parts of western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a home, and there was other damage to the structure. Several trees and power lines were blown down in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed within a highly unstable environment across central Kentucky during the afternoon on April 17th.  One storm remained nearly stationary for an hour over southern LaRue County, bringing localized flash flooding. A similar storm in Madison county sent water over roads in and near Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported water flowing over Cruse Road, located 1 mile north of Maxine. Nearby, at around the same time, a spotter reported water flowing over Parker Grove Road. An unofficial report from the public mentioned that around 5 inches of rain fall within a 2 hour period over an isolated location near Magnolia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man received non life-threatening injuries when he was struck by lightning on a golf course. One of his socks was burned. He was transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on April 16th, a stationary front lay across southern Indiana, parallel to and roughly 75 north of the Ohio River. South of this boundary, very unstable conditions developed during the afternoon, with CAPE exceeding 2000 Joules. By evening, a weak wave moved northeast across southern Indiana along this boundary. Thunderstorms developed across northern Kentucky during the evening hours. Moderate unidirectional shear was present, leading to the development of several supercells with mid-level rotation. Several of these storms produced copious amounts of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Louisville broadcast media reported 1 inch hail 2 miles northeast of Churchhill Downs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual cut-off low developed over Arkansas on Saturday, May 4th. The very slow movement of this feature across Tennessee allowed persistent rain to fall over a two day period from May 4th to May 5th.  All of central Kentucky received over 2 inches of rain over this time span, with several counties such as Taylor, LaRue, Warren, and Adair, totaling over 3 inches. Minor flooding developed on the Green River several days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a two day period ending on May 5th brought minor flooding to the Green River at Woodbury.  Flood state at Woodbury is 33 feet.  The Green River crested at 34.8 feet shortly before noon on May 7th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Green River at Paradise. Some low-lying fields and woods near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across eastern Kentucky. The storms formed in a warm and moist air mass ahead of an approaching cold front that was moving out of the central and southern Plains. The storms produced damaging wind gusts along outflow boundaries that moved along the surface out ahead of the storms themselves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down 1 mile north of Hwy 30 on Airport Rd.

All of central Kentucky received over 2 inches of rain over this time span, with several counties such as Taylor, LaRue, Warren, and Adair, totaling over 3 inches.
