EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from May 4th to May 5th lead to some minor flooding on the Green River at Rochester Ferry several days later.  Flood stage at Rochester is 17 feet.  The river crested at 17.7 feet during the early morning hours on the 5th of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable conditions combined with a weak disturbance dropping southward across the Lower Ohio Valley brought scattered thunderstorms to central and eastern Kentucky during the afternoon. Two storms in particular developed weak mid-level rotation and produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported golf-ball sized hail in Hodgenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of distinct rounds of heavy rain during late February and mid-March caused minor flooding along the Ohio and Green Rivers. Between March 14th and 18th, copious amounts of rain fell, with totals ranging from 1 to 2 inches. A few isolated thunderstorms pushed these rain amounts up to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Ohio River in Henderson County. Some low-lying fields were underwater. A few river access roads were underwater. At the end of a secondary road near the confluence of the Green and Ohio Rivers, a 70-year-old man drowned after driving into floodwaters. The man was discovered by a sheriff's deputy on March 24 while on routine patrol. The water was close to chest deep when the deputy located him. It appeared the victim's truck had stalled in the water and that he attempted to walk out. The victim did not have a phone in his possession. County dispatch was notified of his stalled truck on March 22, but nobody was found during a search by rescue squad personnel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather service employee reported one inch hail in Hodgenville. A spotter also reported hail at around this same time 2 miles northwest of Hodgenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable conditions combined with a weak disturbance dropping southward across the Lower Ohio Valley brought scattered thunderstorms to central and eastern Kentucky during the afternoon. Two storms in particular developed weak mid-level rotation and produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed a report of one inch hail in Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the evening on April 27th brought widespread 2 to 3 inch rains.  The Green River at Rochester Ferry flooded several day later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on April 27 brought minor flooding to the Green River at Rochester on May 1st.  Just after midnight on May 1st, the river was already in flood, having exceeded its flood stage of 17 feet during the early morning hours on April 30th.  It crested at 17.8 feet around noon on May 1st, falling below flood stage by 6 am on May 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave moved across southern Kentucky on May 21st. During the early afternoon hours, a broken line of storms developed along Interstate 65 near Bowling Green. This line expanded to the north and south as it moved across central Kentucky during the mid-afternoon. By late afternoon the northern end of this line moved through Lexington and the southern Bluegrass Region. Strong instability combined with moderate winds aloft brought isolated damaging winds to central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lexington media reported several trees down across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave moved across southern Kentucky on May 21st. During the early afternoon hours, a broken line of storms developed along Interstate 65 near Bowling Green. This line expanded to the north and south as it moved across central Kentucky during the mid-afternoon. By late afternoon the northern end of this line moved through Lexington and the southern Bluegrass Region. Strong instability combined with moderate winds aloft brought isolated damaging winds to central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter relayed a report of several trees down along Tate's Creek Road south of Lexington. Scattered power outages developed around this neighborhood as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved across the Lower Ohio Valley on May 22nd. Several broken lines of convection brought gusty showers to central and eastern Kentucky during the afternoon hours.  One storm became severe across Barren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported that a few trees were downed northwest of Glasgow on New Bowling Green Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and remained quasi-stationary in association with a surface low pressure center over the Mississippi River between St. Louis, Missouri and Cairo, Illinois. The storms were the result of the combination of the surface low, daytime heating, and a very moist atmosphere with precipitable water values approaching two inches. The storms were further enhanced by a 500 mb shortwave trough over southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported up to three inches deep over roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cyclone that began to form over Kansas during the early morning hours on November 17th deepened rapidly to near 980mb as it raced north northeast over the eastern tip of Michigan's upper peninsula by late evening. An unseasonal combination of temperatures in the lower 70s with lower 60s dewpoints overspread all of Illinois,  Indiana, and Kentucky during the early afternoon. A strong jet aloft and very high helicities lead to one of the worst November tornado outbreaks ever, with several dozen tornadoes noted over Illinois and Indiana. However, across much of Kentucky and southern Indiana, widespread cloudiness held temperatures in the mid 60s and severely limited surface-based instability during the afternoon.||In west central Kentucky, a broken line of storms developed. Some of these developed persistent rotation and brought several tornadoes to the western portion of the state. One circulation moved into Butler County, and eventually produced an EF-1 tornado across the central portion of the county. ||Across northern Kentucky, along the Ohio River, widespread soaking rains developed during the pre-dawn hours and continued through mid-morning. Heavy tropical showers trained over a strip of counties right along the Ohio River, including Jefferson, Meade, and Oldham Counties. Some locations received locally over 3 inches of rain in a 5 hour period. Some minor urban flooding inconvenienced several motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with maximum winds of 105 mph first touched down just west of Huntsville, located in Butler County.  Damage was first surveyed on Blaine Road where several trees were uprooted.  While moving across Huntsville along Huntsville-Quality road, it damaged a few homes and uprooted additional trees.  Along Silver City-Huntsville road, additional homes and outbuildings were damaged.  A man was injured within a double wide mobile home when its roof and a wall were lifted from the structure.  This injury occurred just west of the intersection of Panther Creek and Silver City-Huntsville Road.  Finally, farther east along Muddy Creek Road, the tornado damaged several additional homes and trees before lifting 4 mile east of Huntsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds that occurred ahead of a strong cold front brought down trees across eastern Kentucky during the evening and early overnight hours of Halloween 2013.  A line of severe thunderstorms moved into the eastern Bluegrass just prior to midnight, downing trees in Montgomery County.  This line of storms produced additional wind damage across eastern Kentucky after midnight on November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees were blown down on KY233 near DeWitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere lifted northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. This disturbance ushered in warmer air aloft. Rain fell into a shallow layer of sub-freezing air near the surface, resulting in freezing rain. Where the layer of sub-freezing air was deeper, the precipitation fell as sleet. In the Tennessee border area from Murray eastward to Hopkinsville and Elkton, around one-quarter inch of ice coated all surfaces. Trees and power lines were ice-covered. Roads were ice-covered and hazardous. Scattered power outages were reported in the Murray area. Across the remainder of western Kentucky away from the Murray to Hopkinsville corridor, ice accumulations were mainly one-tenth inch or less.

A surface low pressure system deepened as it moved northeast across western Tennessee, reaching the Lower Ohio Valley in the evening. A strong, negatively tilted shortwave at 500 mb ejected northeast from the southern Plains, providing strong forcing for ascent. A squall line organized near the Mississippi River and then surged east-northeast in association with the strengthening surface low. Unseasonably warm and moist air advanced across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana ahead of the squall line. Though instability was weak, it was based near the ground. Very strong low-level wind shear supported embedded circulations within the squall line. These resulted in pockets of very damaging winds and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One barn was blown off its foundation. The roof was blown off another barn.

During the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm developed along a quasi-stationary front that extended from southwest to northeast along the Lower Ohio River Valley. Deep and strong southwesterly wind flow stretched from Texas to the Great Lakes region ahead of an expansive upper-level trough over the western and central states. Several weak disturbances tracked northeastward, contributing to the development of the isolated supercell. Strong low and mid-level wind fields supported the formation of the supercell, which spawned an isolated EF-2 tornado in Livingston County.|Later on during the night, more storms developed in a region of strong warm advection near a low level jet in excess of 50 knots. Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees up to three feet in diameter were blown down across portions of the south side of Paducah. A tree landed on a day care facility. At least a few homes had some shingles blown off. A couple of miles south of Lone Oak, there was damage at a nursery. Tree limbs were blown down, and a barn and greenhouse were damaged.

Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began on the southwest side of town and travelled northeast along Highway 838. Peak winds were estimated around 120 mph. The average path width was 175 yards. The sanctuary of a church collapsed. The steeple was blown off a second church, and some shingles were damaged. Several trees were blown down or snapped. A few homes received shingle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare November winter storm moved through the coal fields on November 26 and 27. Rain chanced to snow during the evening of November 26th and the snow continued through the day on the 27th before tapering off during the evening.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a few tenths of an inch to over four inches over the higher elevations of southeast Kentucky near the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Knott and Floyd County State Highway Garages reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow had accumulated east of Topmost along Highway 7 and Highway 1091 in extreme southeast Knott and southern Floyd counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of rain and wet snow occurred across southeast Kentucky during the evening hours of November 25th.  While snow amounts were generally less than a half inch and impacts were minimal in the lower elevations, snow accumulated rapidly over the higher elevations along the Virginia border stranding several vehicles for a time on Highway 23 over Pine Mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow led to numerous commercial vehicles getting stranded on US Hwy 23 near Jenkins.

A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down across the county.

Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

In the Tennessee border area from Murray eastward to Hopkinsville and Elkton, around one-quarter inch of ice coated all surfaces. Trees and power lines were ice-covered. Roads were ice-covered and hazardous. Scattered power outages were reported in the Murray area. Across the remainder of western Kentucky away from the Murray to Hopkinsville corridor, ice accumulations were mainly one-tenth inch or less.

The majority of the precipitation fell as freezing rain with up to 1/2 ice accumulation observed in Perry, Bell, Rockcastle, Clay and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1/4 of ice was reported around Island City with less amounts to the north.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice amounts ranged from 1/10 in Monticello up to 1/4 on the east end of the county near the McCreary County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm developed along a quasi-stationary front that extended from southwest to northeast along the Lower Ohio River Valley. Deep and strong southwesterly wind flow stretched from Texas to the Great Lakes region ahead of an expansive upper-level trough over the western and central states. Several weak disturbances tracked northeastward, contributing to the development of the isolated supercell. Strong low and mid-level wind fields supported the formation of the supercell, which spawned an isolated EF-2 tornado in Livingston County.|Later on during the night, more storms developed in a region of strong warm advection near a low level jet in excess of 50 knots. Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported multiple state roads closed due to flooding. Two individuals were rescued from the top of a flooded vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing rain created hazardous travel conditions. The highest accumulations of freezing rain were in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Slightly less than one-quarter inch of ice was reported in Todd County. One-eighth inch of ice was reported in Muhlenberg County. Very icy conditions on Kentucky Highway 2551 in Muhlenberg County caused two state salt trucks to slide off the road into ditches. In Henderson and Hopkins Counties, multiple vehicle crashes and slide-offs occurred. Henderson County sheriff deputies worked 12 vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Some of the accidents involved overturned vehicles. There were no serious injuries. The Pennyrile Parkway and Western Kentucky Parkway were two of the major roads that were reported to be very slick. Several vehicles slid off the Western Kentucky Parkway in Hopkins County. Traffic was reduced to one lane on the Western Kentucky Parkway between Dawson Springs and the Pennyrile Parkway interchange. In the Paducah area, there were numerous reports of slips and falls on icy walkways. Most schools were closed for the day. Areas of freezing rain and sleet occurred north of a warm front as it moved north across Tennessee.

During the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm developed along a quasi-stationary front that extended from southwest to northeast along the Lower Ohio River Valley. Deep and strong southwesterly wind flow stretched from Texas to the Great Lakes region ahead of an expansive upper-level trough over the western and central states. Several weak disturbances tracked northeastward, contributing to the development of the isolated supercell. Strong low and mid-level wind fields supported the formation of the supercell, which spawned an isolated EF-2 tornado in Livingston County.|Later on during the night, more storms developed in a region of strong warm advection near a low level jet in excess of 50 knots. Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deep water was flowing across a couple of roads west of Paducah, including State Highway 305. Highway 994 was shut down in Paducah due to flash flooding. A vehicle that was unable to navigate a flooded intersection in Paducah ended up in a flooded ditch. In Paducah, floodwaters from Crooked Creek overtopped a bridge, closing the road. A garage was flooded in an urban area in Paducah. The 24-hour rainfall at the National Weather Service office in Paducah was 4.61 inches. A total of 1.40 inches fell within 40 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of barns and other farm structures were levelled or heavily damaged. A silo and three greenhouses were blown down. Windows were blown out of a building. Dozens of trees were uprooted. The damage swath averaged 300 yards wide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The path of damage averaged about one-third mile wide. The downburst damage exited the northeast corner of Todd County into Muhlenberg County near Lake Malone State Park. A few thousand trees were uprooted by winds estimated near 110 mph. Numerous barns and other sheds were levelled or heavily damaged. Several homes and other buildings had roofs, including decking, partially removed. A couple of homes received damage to walls of attached garages. Many other homes received minor damage, including loss of shingles and siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air on the backside of a departing low pressure system interacted with surface moisture to create freezing rain on January 15, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four tenths of an inch of ice was observed on tree branches. Tree branches were heard cracking due to the weight of the ice. The accumulated ice had little impact and was confined to higher elevation areas throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 1.5 inches of rain fell on saturated ground. The heavy rain was associated with a low pressure system over the Plains states. A strong southerly low level wind flow ahead of a cold front provided abundant moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over a secondary road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the region, bringing strong winds aloft across the Ohio Valley. Showers that developed along and ahead of a cold front associated with this storm system were capable of producing damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes. This line of showers continued past midnight on October 31st and into the early morning hours of November 1st.

A strong low pressure system combined with an unseasonably warm airmass to produce organized storms across the region. These storms were tornadic across Illinois and western Indiana, and began to transition to non-tornadic storms as they entered northern Kentucky. The main threat from these storms when they moved across northern Kentucky was damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top of a tree broke off and landed on a car due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare November winter storm moved through the coal fields on November 26 and 27. Rain chanced to snow during the evening of November 26th and the snow continued through the day on the 27th before tapering off during the evening.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a few tenths of an inch to over four inches over the higher elevations of southeast Kentucky near the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell above 2400 feet elevation across Letcher County. A trained spotter at 2400 feet elevation reported 3.9 inches of new snow as of 9:38 AM EST on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system deepened as it moved northeast across western Tennessee, reaching the Lower Ohio Valley in the evening. A strong, negatively tilted shortwave at 500 mb ejected northeast from the southern Plains, providing strong forcing for ascent. A squall line organized near the Mississippi River and then surged east-northeast in association with the strengthening surface low. Unseasonably warm and moist air advanced across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana ahead of the squall line. Though instability was weak, it was based near the ground. Very strong low-level wind shear supported embedded circulations within the squall line. These resulted in pockets of very damaging winds and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak tornado touched down 2 miles west of Dexter in northern Calloway County. The tornado uprooted a couple of trees and downed power lines during its brief touchdown.

Snowfall amounts ranged from a few tenths of an inch to over four inches over the higher elevations of southeast Kentucky near the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Knott and Floyd County State Highway Garages reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow had accumulated east of Topmost along Highway 7 and Highway 1091 in extreme southeast Knott and southern Floyd counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers across the Ohio Valley brought one to two inches of accumulating snowfall. Cold air funneled into the region and numerous roadways were closed due to accidents from icy buildups, particularly in the Cincinnati metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter south of Walton measured 1.5 inches of snow. The Cincinnati airport measured 1.1 inches. Late Thursday, Interstate 75 was shut down northbound in Boone County at the I-71/I-75 split and reopened just after 11:30 p.m. Interstate 275 in Boone County was shut down at mile marker 11 near Hebron. The highway was closed in both directions because of the accidents. The bridge across the Ohio River at the Kentucky-Indiana border was also closed.

A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere lifted northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. This disturbance ushered in warmer air aloft. Rain fell into a shallow layer of sub-freezing air near the surface, resulting in freezing rain. Where the layer of sub-freezing air was deeper, the precipitation fell as sleet. In the Tennessee border area from Murray eastward to Hopkinsville and Elkton, around one-quarter inch of ice coated all surfaces. Trees and power lines were ice-covered. Roads were ice-covered and hazardous. Scattered power outages were reported in the Murray area. Across the remainder of western Kentucky away from the Murray to Hopkinsville corridor, ice accumulations were mainly one-tenth inch or less.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto Hwy 2227.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The downburst damage path entered Muhlenberg County from the northeast corner of Todd County. The path of damage averaged about one-third mile wide. A few thousand trees were uprooted by winds estimated near 110 mph. Some barns and other sheds were levelled or heavily damaged. Several homes and other buildings had roofs, including decking, partially removed. A couple of homes received damage to walls of attached garages. Many other homes received minor damage, including loss of shingles and siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cyclone began to deepen over Illinois during the afternoon hours on October 31st. Toward midnight, as low pressure moved across southern Lake Michigan, it deepened to well below 990mb. A powerful low level jet developed over the Lower Ohio Valley by mid-afternoon, as winds at 925mb increased to around 55kt by evening. These winds translated to surface winds of around 30 mph with frequent gusts up to 50 mph across southern Indiana and the Commonwealth.  In addition to strong gradient winds, several convective lines developed across central Kentucky during the evening. Despite a lack of thunder, these lines of showers brought additional wind damage across the eastern Bluegrass and Lake Cumberland Regions during the early morning hours of November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Waco fire department reported trees down on Goggins Lane and Redhouse Road. Downed trees also blocked Tates Creek Road, Four Mile Road and Summit Street. Nearby, a Kentucky mesonet site measured a 48 mph wind gust. In Richmond, a weather station located at Hummel Planetarium measured a 56 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere lifted northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. This disturbance ushered in warmer air aloft. A surface low pressure system deepened as it moved northeast across western Tennessee, reaching the Lower Ohio Valley in the evening. A strong, negatively tilted shortwave at 500 mb ejected northeast from the southern Plains, providing strong forcing for ascent. A squall line organized near the Mississippi River and then surged east-northeast in association with the strengthening surface low. Unseasonably warm and moist air advanced across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana ahead of the squall line. Though instability was weak, it was based near the ground. Very strong low-level wind shear supported embedded circulations within the squall line. These resulted in pockets of very damaging winds and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst was oriented in a northeast direction across Kentucky Highway 58, just west of the intersection with Route 301. A trampoline was tossed about 25 yards. Several shingles on the west side of two garages were dislodged. A large dead tree was knocked over. Large tree limbs were down. The damage path width was about 90 yards. The microburst developed along a north to south oriented squall line that moved through eastern Graves County.

On U.S. Highway 641 a few miles north of Murray, a trampoline was blown into the middle of the road. Construction barrels were blown over. The peak wind gust at the Murray/Calloway County airport was 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure quickly developed and moved across southern Kentucky during the morning hours of January 25th, sending a wintry mix into southern Kentucky. The majority of the precipitation fell as freezing rain with up to 1/2 ice accumulation observed in Perry, Bell, Rockcastle, Clay and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1/2 of ice was reported 1 mile southwest of Livingston, with 1/8 reported in Mt. Vernon and the remainder of the county. Several accidents were reported on I-75 and other county roads during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, a supercell thunderstorm developed along a quasi-stationary front that extended from southwest to northeast along the Lower Ohio River Valley. Deep and strong southwesterly wind flow stretched from Texas to the Great Lakes region ahead of an expansive upper-level trough over the western and central states. Several weak disturbances tracked northeastward, contributing to the development of the isolated supercell. Strong low and mid-level wind fields supported the formation of the supercell, which spawned an isolated EF-2 tornado in Livingston County.|Later on during the night, more storms developed in a region of strong warm advection near a low level jet in excess of 50 knots. Marginal instability developed as a surface warm front lifted northward. This round of storms produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of secondary roads in the southern part of the county were impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy tropical-like showers began Saturday, October 5th and continued through Sunday morning.  This episodic convection developed within an unseasonably moist airmass south and east of a stalled boundary.  Overall, over a 48 hour period, 5 to locally over 7 inches of rain fall along the Ohio River from Louisville southwest to Owensboro, Kentucky.  Louisville International Airport (KSDF), the official climate station for Louisville, received 7.52 inches of rain in three days, including 5.91 inches on October 5th alone.  Even Lexington, located in the Bluegrass Region, totaled around 4 inches over the 5th and 6th.  The bulk of this rain fell within a 6 hour period from Saturday evening through 1 am Sunday morning.  Just under 4 inches fell across the Louisville metropolitan area during this time, stranding many automobiles and leading to several water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lanes were blocked by around 1 foot of flowing water near the intersection of Shelbyville Road and Blankenbaker Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the region, bringing strong winds aloft across the Ohio Valley. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large limbs were downed in Maysville due to thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, damaging a couple of vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased over southeast Missouri and southern Illinois within a moist and unstable environment ahead of an advancing cold front moving across southeast Missouri. Rainfall amounts ahead of the front were between 1 and 3 inches, although there were scattered amounts between 4 and 5 inches.

Overall, over a 48 hour period, 5 to locally over 7 inches of rain fall along the Ohio River from Louisville southwest to Owensboro, Kentucky.  Louisville International Airport (KSDF), the official climate station for Louisville, received 7.52 inches of rain in three days, including 5.91 inches on October 5th alone.  Even Lexington, located in the Bluegrass Region, totaled around 4 inches over the 5th and 6th.  The bulk of this rain fell within a 6 hour period from Saturday evening through 1 am Sunday morning.  Just under 4 inches fell across the Louisville metropolitan area during this time, stranding many automobiles and leading to several water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This severe weather episode is a continuation of the one that began on the evening of August 31. A short line of storms accelerated southward across the Pennyrile region. These storms were in advance of a weak cold front that extended from central Indiana westward along Interstate 70 to the Mississippi River. The storms moved through a moderately unstable air mass with mixed-layer capes between 1000 and 2000 j/kg. A large area of stratiform rain in the wake of the squall line caused some minor flooding in the Henderson area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Bremen, including one that landed on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure lifted north through the Chicago and the Lake Michigan vicinity during the late afternoon on Sunday the 17th.  A strong low level wind field existed in the Ohio Valley.  Extension clouds and showers fell during the day on Sunday well ahead of the associated cold front.  Surface surface dew points raised into the upper 50 degrees by nightfall on Sunday evening.  ||As the parent low pressure center lifted north toward Ontario, the associated front swept through northeast Kentucky during the evening hours.  Narrow bands of showers, with just a few embedded thunderstorms, preceded the front.  The showers were moving at 50 to 60 mph.  Widespread wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph occurred.  A stronger gust was reported in Greenup County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cyclone began to deepen over Illinois during the afternoon hours on October 31st. Towards midnight, as low pressure moved across southern Lake Michigan, it deepened to well below 990mb. A powerful low level jet developed over the Lower Ohio Valley by mid-afternoon, as winds at 925mb increased to around 55kt by evening. These winds translated to surface winds of around 30 mph with frequent gusts up to 50 mph across southern Indiana and the Commonwealth. Widespread minor wind damage developed as large branches fall across roads and power lines. In addition to strong gradient winds, several convective lines of showers developed by mid-evening across central Kentucky. Despite a lack of thunder, these lines of showers brought additional wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported that winds associated with a strong line of showers brought damage to his storm doors and his garage. In his neighborhood many large tree limbs were downed, causing power outages. Thirteen minutes earlier, just 3 miles northwest of Winchester, a Kentucky mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 57 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased over southeast Missouri and southern Illinois within a moist and unstable environment ahead of an advancing cold front moving across southeast Missouri. Low level winds strengthened as the storms moved east across the Lower Ohio Valley, resulting in isolated strong wind gusts. The air mass ahead of the front was warm and humid, reminiscent of late summer. Moisture was in abundance, and storms were very efficient rain makers. Rainfall amounts ahead of the front were between 1 and 3 inches, although there were scattered amounts between 4 and 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated between 55 and 60 mph by law enforcement personnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough embedded within the southern stream of the jet combined with unseasonably rich moisture to bring widespread flooding and a regional severe weather outbreak to the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening of December 21st.  Heavy rains, on the order of 2 to locally as much as 3 inches, developed during the morning hours across southern Indiana and the Kentucky counties along the Ohio River. These heavy rains, augmented by a December record 1.7 inches of precipitable water, developed along a stationary front that lay along but just north of the Ohio River from Cairo, IL to Cincinnati. These early rains, combined with thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening, brought areal and river flooding to north central Kentucky. ||By early afternoon, low pressure deepened as it moved northeast across central Illinois and northern Indiana. Rains ceased by mid-day as central Kentucky fell within the warm sector of the developing cyclone. Record high temperatures in the lower 70s arrived by early evening.  A severe squall line developed by mid-afternoon from southern Illinois southwards along the Mississippi River. This line of storms, fueled by ample moisture and an exceptionally strong low level jet in excess of 50 kts, became severe as it moved across southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the evening hours. Three circulations within this line produced tornadoes across central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region. For the Louisville weather office, these 3 December tornadoes were the first observed since 1971.  This line also brought widespread damaging winds, especially to the Bluegrass Region and Hart and Barren Counties in Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey confirmed that an EF-1 tornado was embedded within a severe squall line that brought widespread damage to northern Bourbon County.  An area of concentrated damage was surveyed along Colville and Steele Ford Roads as well as Endicott Lane. Along these roads several large barns and outbuildings were either destroyed or heavily damaged. EF-1 damage was confirmed along Endicott Lane as some well-anchored barn footers were lifted up and thrown 75 yards.  Several residents also reported a loud roar within torrential rain as the line of storms passed.

At the National Weather Service office just west of Paducah, 2.35 inches of rain fell in an hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage began just south of the intersection of U.S. Highway 62 and County Road 1015. At that location, a very large but shallow-rooted tree was uprooted. A glass door to a home was destroyed. This home along with other homes in the immediate area had minor roof and siding damage. A mobile home was blown off its foundation. Two sheds were destroyed. Further northeast, along the south side of U.S. Highway 62, there were several homes with shingles missing and one home with the garage door blown in. On the north side of Highway 62, a large well-built barn sustained partial roof loss. Following U.S. Highway 62 northeast, there were many trees snapped or uprooted, large branches broken off, and bean fields flattened. An amateur radio tower was bent over, and more homes had minor roof damage. A mobile home was flipped over. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph. The tornado path continued northeast into McCracken County just south of Lovelaceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was located over Indiana during the early afternoon hours on Monday, June 10th. Broad cyclonic flow aloft south of this feature brought brisk westerly mid and upper level winds to southern Kentucky and Tennessee. Despite relatively weak instability and moisture availability, cool temperatures aloft and steep mid-level lapse rates lead to the development of several low-topped supercells across the southern portions of the Commonwealth and central Tennessee.  One supercell developed just to the south of Russellville, spawning large hail and a strong tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed a strong, long-tracked tornado that first touched down northwest of Adairville near the intersection of Vick Road and State Highway 96, where a barn sustained roof damage and another outbuilding was destroyed.  This tornado had a 14 mile long track, 9 of which was in Logan County. Overall, this tornado stayed on the ground for 29 minutes.  Damage consistent with a high-end EF-2 tornado was surveyed from near State Highway 96 to just west of the Logan Simpson County line. The worst damage occurred to the north-northeast of Adairville, where the twister crossed Highway 663. Several homes suffered extensive damage. Three people were injured while taking shelter in the bathroom of one home.  A fourth injury occurred at a residence along Trimble Road.  The tornado weakened   as it crossed into Simpson County just north of the intersection of Conn and Reames-Dixon Roads.

Just under 4 inches fell across the Louisville metropolitan area during this time, stranding many automobiles and leading to several water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell across power lines along Standish Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft was well established over the Lower Ohio Valley during the morning hours on August 31st. Just above the surface, light southwesterly winds brought modified Gulf moisture across the Commonwealth and southern Indiana. Strong instability developed by afternoon, even in the presence of morning convection associated with a southward moving outflow boundary that brought strong storms to the Bluegrass Region. After this first round of storms, strong convection developed during the afternoon hours across southern Indiana and central Kentucky east of Interstate 65. Several of these storms became severe as several wet microbursts brought widespread damaging winds to southeastern Indiana and the western Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported several trees down across Russell Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed much of western Kentucky. High pressure centered over the Upper Ohio Valley produced a light east to southeast wind flow of moist air. Very moist ground from a cool and wet pattern during the first half of August contributed to the fog. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less across a widespread area south and west of the Henderson and Owensboro regions. A serious vehicle accident in Graves County was blamed on the foggy conditions. One man was hurt in the two-vehicle accident on State Route 80 east of Mayfield. The injuries were not life-threatening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of frontal boundaries and a weak shortwave trough, combined with ample low and middle level moisture, led to the formation of scattered to numerous heavy rainfall producing showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of August 13, 2013. Significant flooding occurred across portions of Powell and Floyd counties as a result of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local elementary school was flooded when over 6 inches of water entered the building. Nearby KY Hwy 680 was also closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated instance of flooding occurred in Rockcastle county on August 16, 2013. The storm that produced the flooding formed in a warm and unstable air mass ahead of an approaching upper level weather system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across Hwy 2250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow moving strong thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Kentucky on August 21. The storms formed in a warm and very unstable air mass south of a slow moving frontal boundary. Significant flooding was reported in Wolfe, Powell, and Floyd counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Campbell Hill Rd and Plummers St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms organized into a small bow echo as they crossed extreme southern Illinois, then moved east across the Ohio River into western Kentucky. The storms occurred along a cold front as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. The activity was supported by a mesoscale vorticity center that was over southeast Missouri. Moderately strong southwest winds aloft (around 30 knots in the mid levels) contributed to storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over some roads in Central City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours across central Kentucky ahead of a surface trough that moved through early the next morning. Despite limited instability, precipitable water values approaching 2 inches and light winds aloft led to some heavy rainfall totals across the Bluegrass Region. One storm brought between 2 and 3 inches of rain over a 2 hour period to Woodford County, located just west of Lexington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public sent an image showing water approaching at least one home on High View Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late evening on August 7th, centered over the Kentucky Bluegrass Region. The slow movement of these storms, coupled with a tropical environment in which precipitable water values exceeded 2 inches, led to several very localized instances of flash flooding. The worst flooding developed over Anderson and Shelby Counties. Dual Polarization Radar estimated that over 6 inches of rain fell within a 3 hour period near Sinai in Anderson County. Twenty miles to the northwest, radar estimated around 4 inches of rainfall that fell near Southville, in Shelby County.  A Coop observer in Anderson County measured 4.06 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A creek overflowed its banks in Harrodsburg and covered State Highway 127 with two feet of running water. A car became stalled near this overpass.

Locally damaging winds occurred in severe convection along the leading edge of an area of thunderstorms. The storms occurred in a southwest low level wind flow ahead of a surface trough over the Mississippi Valley. A weak 500 mb shortwave trough moving east across Missouri further aided storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst downed dozens of trees and limbs. A carport was blown approximately 400 feet and destroyed. The average width of the damage path was 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow moving strong thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Kentucky on August 21. The storms formed in a warm and very unstable air mass south of a slow moving frontal boundary. Significant flooding was reported in Wolfe, Powell, and Floyd counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water was flowing across Main St. in several places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined and progressive nearly cutoff low was positioned over east central Indiana during the afternoon of August 21st.  South and east of this feature, a seasonally strong cyclonic 300mb jet was present, placing central and eastern Kentucky within an area of diffluence aloft.  Strong instability up to 2000 joules developed by mid-afternoon along and east of Interstate 65.  Scattered thunderstorms developed by late morning and strengthen during the afternoon hours.  Training storms produced, within a 2 hour period, a strip of 4 to as much as 5 inches of rain across northern Lincoln and Garrard Counties.  Within this line of storms, lightning struck a barn, killing two people and injuring three others. This has become Kentucky's worst lightning related incident in several years.  Later, a strong storm that briefly displayed supercell characteristics developed over Shelby County.  This storm produced golfball-sized hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported along Clark Station Road between Fisherville and Eastwood.

The worst flooding developed over Anderson and Shelby Counties. Dual Polarization Radar estimated that over 6 inches of rain fell within a 3 hour period near Sinai in Anderson County. Twenty miles to the northwest, radar estimated around 4 inches of rainfall that fell near Southville, in Shelby County.  A Coop observer in Anderson County measured 4.06 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shelby County Road Department reported that quite a bit of vegetative debris was left on several roads in the morning from flash flooding that developed shortly after midnight.  Two of these were Cap Ridge and Little Crooked Creek, both located around 4 miles north of Mount Eden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined and progressive nearly cutoff low was positioned over east central Indiana during the afternoon of August 21st.  South and east of this feature, a seasonally strong cyclonic 300mb jet was present, placing central and eastern Kentucky within an area of diffluence aloft.  Strong instability up to 2000 joules developed by mid-afternoon along and east of Interstate 65.  Scattered thunderstorms developed by late morning and strengthen during the afternoon hours.  Training storms produced, within a 2 hour period, a strip of 4 to as much as 5 inches of rain across northern Lincoln and Garrard Counties.  Within this line of storms, lightning struck a barn, killing two people and injuring three others. This has become Kentucky's worst lightning related incident in several years.  Later, a strong storm that briefly displayed supercell characteristics developed over Shelby County.  This storm produced golfball-sized hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported two trees down along Peaks Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short line of thunderstorms moved southeast across the Ohio River from Indiana, then accelerated southward across the Pennyrile region. This line of thunderstorms was accompanied by locally damaging winds. These storms were in advance of a weak cold front that extended from central Indiana westward along Interstate 70 to the Mississippi River. The storms moved through a moderately unstable air mass with mixed-layer capes between 1000 and 2000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Kentucky mesonet site measured a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft was well established over the Lower Ohio Valley during the morning hours on August 31st. Just above the surface, light southwesterly winds brought modified Gulf moisture across the Commonwealth and southern Indiana. Strong instability developed by afternoon, even in the presence of morning convection associated with a southward moving outflow boundary that brought strong storms to the Bluegrass Region. After this first round of storms, strong convection developed during the afternoon hours across southern Indiana and central Kentucky east of Interstate 65. Several of these storms became severe as several wet microbursts brought widespread damaging winds to southeastern Indiana and the western Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County emergency manager reported several roads closed due to high water across Lexington. Some of these roads include Tahoe, Tate's Creek,and Parker's Mill Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms organized into a small bow echo as they crossed extreme southern Illinois, then moved east across the Ohio River into western Kentucky. The storms occurred along a cold front as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. The activity was supported by a mesoscale vorticity center that was over southeast Missouri. Moderately strong southwest winds aloft (around 30 knots in the mid levels) contributed to storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cone-shaped funnel cloud was photographed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed much of western Kentucky. High pressure centered over the Upper Ohio Valley produced a light east to southeast wind flow of moist air. Very moist ground from a cool and wet pattern during the first half of August contributed to the fog. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less across a widespread area south and west of the Henderson and Owensboro regions. A serious vehicle accident in Graves County was blamed on the foggy conditions. One man was hurt in the two-vehicle accident on State Route 80 east of Mayfield. The injuries were not life-threatening.
Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms fired up in a warm and moist air mass during the afternoon and evening hours of July 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of frontal boundaries and a weak shortwave trough, combined with ample low and middle level moisture, led to the formation of scattered to numerous heavy rainfall producing showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of August 13, 2013. Significant flooding occurred across portions of Powell and Floyd counties as a result of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total measured rainfall between 204 and 525 pm was 2.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system organized over the Lower Ohio Valley along a southeast-moving cold front. Moderately strong northwest winds aloft and seasonably strong instability contributed to the strength of the system. A gust front along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex accelerated south and southwestward across the northwest Kentucky counties of Union and Henderson. This gust front produced areas of wind damage in those counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud with broad, weak rotation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth across northeast Kentucky.  Temperatures reached 70 to 75 degrees.   In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Route 784.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across eastern Kentucky during the overnight and early morning hours of August 13, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was out of creek banks in the area and covering driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall led to flooding across eastern Kentucky on December 6th and 7th. In general, between two and half and three and a half inches of rain fell for the event and a number of roads around the area were underwater from time to time. There were also a few instances of automobiles becoming surrounded by water.  The rain transitioned to a wintry mix late on the 6th before ending and this led to a few automobile accidents in Fleming county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch or two of water was running across Old Brodhead Road near the bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed late in the afternoon into the evening hours in a very warm, moist and unstable air mass across eastern Kentucky during the 4th of July Holiday.  Several of these thunderstorms became severe and downed trees and brought flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Jetts Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moveds across eastern Kentucky during the morning hours. In advance of this front, a line of severe thunderstorms raced across the area producing numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado in Pulaski County. Moderate to heavy rainfall lingered over far southeast Kentucky, causing some minor flooding later into the day and through the night for parts of Floyd, Letcher, and Harlan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was blown down in Hueysville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down onto Hwy 229 and several other roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front in the area and a mid level disturbance helped generate an organized band of thunderstorms in Kentucky during the afternoon.  That large cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through eastern Kentucky during the early evening hours.  The low level flow backed around to the southeast.  As a result, a few stronger cells, on the southern edge of this convection, were able to maintain their strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado descended a steep hillside from the county border.  The storm then went across a small narrow valley containing Youngs Branch to the adjacent hilltop.  ||Peak wind gusts were estimated by the survey team at 75 mph. ||Several trees were snapped off or broken coming down the initial hillside. The 2 houses on the second hilltop sustained minor damage to soffits.  One half of a barn door was ripped off.  There was more soffit damage to the barn.   Between the 2 residences, a large metal car port was ripped off and thrown several yards.  A large fenced garden was also damaged.  There was no hail, but leaves were ripped off the trees and other vegetation.  The leaves were left pasted onto all sides of the homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure drew an unseasonably warm and moist air mass across the region. Convection organized ahead of the low and brought heavy rainfall and damaging winds to the area from the evening of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two roads were closed due to high water over typical low lying locations due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper wave moved across the Great Lakes overnight and through the morning on June 13th.  Low pressure associated with this system deepened as it quickly moved across Ohio during the morning hours.  Although most of the severe weather earlier that morning tracked well north across Indiana and Ohio, a thin line of convection developed along a sharp cold front.  This boundary, and associated thunderstorms, moved southeast through southern Indiana and eventually the Kentucky Bluegrass Region around mid-morning.  Despite the time of day, a strong westerly low level jet and steep low level lapse rates allowed frequent wind gusts from 40 to 50 mph all along this line, as well as more isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jamestown local fire department reported a tree blown on a house trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very humid airmass with dewpoints well into the lower 70s was in place over the Lower Ohio Valley during the late morning and early afternoon on July 10th.  A sharpening trough over the western Great Lakes brought moderate mid-level northwest flow over the region during the afternoon hours.  Strong instability and the absence of a cap allowed individual strong storms to develop by early afternoon across northern Kentucky along Interstate 64.  Some of these storms brought localized damaging wind gusts.  A cold pool driven short bowing segment crossed Harrison and Floyd counties in Indiana during the mid afternoon hours, eventually crossing the Ohio River and entering Louisville. This bowing segment brought widespread damaging winds to Louisville, including a 64 mph measured wind gust at Standiford Field in Louisville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the intersection of Kidville Lane and Jacks Creek Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow moving strong thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding across portions of eastern Kentucky on August 21. The storms formed in a warm and very unstable air mass south of a slow moving frontal boundary. Significant flooding was reported in Wolfe, Powell, and Floyd counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was flown off of a home on James Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system that originated over the Missouri Ozarks maintained strength as it moved through the middle Mississippi Valley during the overnight hours. The system slowed down and then stalled as it crossed the Lower Ohio Valley very early in the morning. The stalled thunderstorm complex produced major flash flooding across parts of western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Highway 58 was closed by floodwaters at the 10 mile marker near Clinton. Kentucky 1820 was closed by floodwaters at the Graves-Hickman County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms downed trees and power lines and produced large hail for portions of eastern KY during the afternoon hours of July 13. A westward moving upper level area of low pressure combined with abundant low level moisture to produce strong to severe thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported to have been blown down onto trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An usually warm and moist air mass settled over eastern KY on December 21st and 22nd. Temperatures during the overnight hours were in the lower 60s for the entire area. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the area ahead of a fast moving and very strong cold front. These storms caused widespread wind damage across the area and frequently produced wind gusts in excess of 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, including one that landed on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across eastern Kentucky. The storms formed in a warm and moist air mass ahead of an approaching cold front that was moving out of the central and southern Plains. The storms produced damaging wind gusts along outflow boundaries that moved along the surface out ahead of the storms themselves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down in Poplarville on Hwy 3269.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall led to flooding across eastern Kentucky on December 6th and 7th. In general, between two and half and three and a half inches of rain fell for the event and a number of roads around the area were underwater from time to time. There were also a few instances of automobiles becoming surrounded by water.  The rain transitioned to a wintry mix late on the 6th before ending and this led to a few automobile accidents in Fleming county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A yard was under water on Williams Street near the Mount Vernon Elementary School. The water had no quite reached the house, but was up to the edge of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms passed through eastern Kentucky during the early hours of November 1st along a strong cold front.  The storms produced wind gusts up to 63 mph, blowing down trees and causing numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in and around Somerset. A power pole was snapped off causing power lines to come down onto vehicles parked nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed within a highly unstable environment across central Kentucky during the afternoon on April 17th.  One storm remained nearly stationary for an hour over southern LaRue County, bringing localized flash flooding. A similar storm in Madison county sent water over roads in and near Richmond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 6 miles west-southwest of Hodgenville reported one inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms passed through eastern Kentucky during the early hours of November 1st along a strong cold front.  The storms produced wind gusts up to 63 mph, blowing down trees and causing numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three big trees were downed along with electrical and fiber optic lines at Gladie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: United States Hwy 23, both northbound and southbound lanes, were blocked due to high water near mile marker 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably moist air, combined with a nearly stationary boundary draped from Michigan through western Tennessee, lead to several days of heavy rain across west central and central Kentucky. Over a 4 day period from late Thursday, January 10th through Sunday the 14th, 3 to 4 inches of rain were recorded. The highest amounts, around two and one half inches, fell along the Ohio River southwest of Louisville over a 15 hour period ending Sunday afternoon.  This led to some very minor flooding across low water crossings and open fields.  Several rivers experienced minor flooding on Sunday and Monday, January 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain led to minor flooding on the Green River at Rochester Ferry. Flood stage at Rochester Ferry is 17 feet. The Green River crested at 18.6 feet during the evening hours on January 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with record warmth across northeast Kentucky.  Temperatures reached 70 to 75 degrees.   In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Chinns Branch and Route 693.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front, ample low level moisture, and abundant instability combined to bring severe weather to portions of eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of April 11th. The bulk of the severe weather occurred in Bath and Montgomery counties, with isolated severe events also occurring in Rockcastle, Laurel, Rowan, and Whitley counties. Significant wind damage occurred in Montgomery county between Camargo and Jeffersonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the Woodbine community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified as it moved east from Missouri. The storms increased as they moved into slightly more unstable air associated with a strengthening low level jet. The storms were aided by the approach of a strong upper level trough. Despite weak instability, winds of 90 knots in the lowest 3 kilometers coupled with extreme shear values resulted in damaging winds and isolated tornadoes with the strongest storms. Ahead of the thunderstorms, strong southerly gradient winds gusted up to 45 mph, mainly along and southeast of a line from Owensboro to Murray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several barns were levelled or heavily damaged. Center pivot irrigation equipment was overturned. Several homes received shingle and siding damage. Fifteen power poles were snapped along U.S. Highway 41A. Dozens of trees were uprooted. A semi-trailer rig was overturned along Interstate 24 near mile marker 89. The entire community of Pembroke was without power. There was considerable debris along Highway 115. The swath of damage from this downburst averaged one mile wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms moved north across western Kentucky. The storms occurred in a southwesterly flow of moist and moderately unstable air. The storms were enhanced by a 500 mb shortwave extending from the Ozarks to the Lower Mississippi Valley. A few of the storms produced large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail occurred on the western edge of Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small, low-topped thunderstorm line segments moved northeast in a region of strong vertical wind shear and very weak instability. These line segments produced a few short-track, weak tornadoes and isolated wind damage. The tornadoes formed from mesovortices embedded within the line. There was little if any lightning associated with the convection. The storms occurred along a cold front that extended southward from a low pressure center in northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued from Graves County into Marshall County. There was shingle and soffit damage to a few homes. Several trees were snapped, and large branches were blown down. The average damage path width was 150 yards, and the peak winds were about 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous amplified pattern for summer featured a nearly stationary deep 500mb closed low over western Kentucky. Cold temperatures aloft combined with seasonally average moisture led to the development of numerous storms during the early morning hours on July 1st. Several storms developed near Bowling Green and brought over 2 inches to the Bowling Green Airport around dawn. Additional convection developed during the mid to late afternoon hours. Many of these storms, forming right underneath the cold 500mb temperatures, were slow moving and strong. A few brought episodes of flash flooding and marginally severe hail, concentrated over the Kentucky Bluegrass Region and south central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported quarter sized hail at Mile Marker 7 on Interstate 65.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across eastern Kentucky during the overnight and early morning hours of August 13, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people had to be rescued from a flooded house in the Slick Rock Branch area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of extreme northern Kentucky during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed due to high water caused by heavy rainfall in the Covington area including 12th Street.
